summary: This cluster of papers investigated the evolutionary origins and phylogenetic relationships of photosynthetic eukaryotes, with a particular focus on the systematics of diverse algal groups. The research employed mitochondrial cristae architecture as a key morphological character to construct a speculative phylogeny, which placed golden algae within a "stramenopile" clade and dinoflagellates within an "alveolate" clade, while also examining the positions of jakobid flagellates and euglenoids. A central finding was the critical role of heterotrophic protists in the emergence of photosynthetic lineages, emphasizing that understanding algal evolution requires tracing connections between non-photosynthetic ancestors and modern plastid-bearing groups. The geographic scope was not explicitly defined, but the work addressed broad, global-scale questions in eukaryotic evolution rather than region-specific ecological patterns.
